planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; black medic is adapted to a wide range of soils including thin, gravelly, and moderately fertile soils where other legume cover crops fail. Can be sown in fall in the South for winter ground cover.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed in well-drained to dry, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; black medic is a small annual or short-lived perennial clover relative naturalized from Europe, commonly found growing as a lawn weed in dry, thin, alkaline soils where it is actually performing a valuable service — fixing atmospheric nitrogen into plant-available form through root nodule bacteria. As a cover crop or green manure, it is valuable on poor, dry, alkaline soils where white clover (Trifolium repens) performs poorly. The tiny yellow flowers are important early-season nectar sources for small native bees. Black medic is sometimes included in wildflower and bee-forage seed mixes. It self-sows readily and can be used as a living mulch under fruit trees on dry, alkaline soils. Mow or till before seed set to prevent unwanted spreading.

planting.note: Scarify seeds (nick seed coat or soak 24 hours in hot water) and direct sow after last frost in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; trailing indigo grows naturally in dry, sandy, open pine flatwoods and coastal habitats from Florida to Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; trailing indigo is a low-growing native legume of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, forming a prostrate to semi-erect mound of attractive pinnate foliage with numerous small, pink to salmon-rose flowers throughout the warm season. The plant is an important larval host for the Ceraunus blue butterfly and several other hairstreaks that feed on native legumes, and the flowers attract small native bees throughout the summer. Extremely drought tolerant and heat tolerant once established in appropriate dry, sandy soils. In zone 9a it often persists as a short-lived perennial, dying back after hard freezes and resprouting from the root. A valuable pollinator plant for dry, sandy, longleaf pine ecosystem-style garden designs.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, poorly drained soil in full sun; sensitive joint-vetch is a native tropical legume of wet, seasonally flooded soils from Florida to Texas, naturally colonizing pond margins, wet roadside ditches, and flooded bottomlands.

directions: Direct sow in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in warm to hot conditions after frost danger; sensitive joint-vetch is a native annual legume of wetland margins and seasonally flooded habitats, notable for leaves that fold when touched — the same touch-sensitivity seen in the Mimosa genus. It is a high-value wildlife plant: the seeds are extremely important food for wood ducks and other waterfowl, and the plant is included in waterfowl management plantings across the Gulf South. As a cover crop, it excels in wet or temporarily flooded soils where other legumes cannot survive, fixing nitrogen in anaerobic conditions. The yellow flowers are attractive to small native bees. Used in restoration seeding mixes for wetland and pond-edge habitats in the Gulf Coastal Plain.

planting.note: Scarify seeds and direct sow after last frost in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; tropical sensitive plant grows naturally on dry to moist roadsides, disturbed areas, and open sandy habitats of the Gulf Coastal Plain, often in disturbed sandy sites.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; tropical sensitive plant is a sprawling native legume of the Gulf Coastal Plain with leaves that fold when touched or disturbed — providing an endlessly entertaining tactile experience for children and visitors. The bright yellow, pom-pom-like flower heads are cheerful and attract native bees throughout the warm season. The plant is a native wildflower of open, disturbed sandy habitats, roadsides, and open flatwoods from Florida to Texas, adapting well to hot, dry, sunny garden conditions. A nitrogen-fixing legume that improves soil fertility. The touch-sensitive leaves are sometimes confused with Mimosa pudica (non-native sensitive plant) but Neptunia has the distinctive yellow puff flowers rather than pink. In zone 9a it behaves as a warm-season perennial that dies back in cold winters.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds or plant container-grown seedlings after last frost in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Manila tamarind is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant and thrives on calcareous, alkaline soils of south Texas and Mexico.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Manila tamarind is a thorny, fast-growing legume tree native to Mexico and Central America, now widely naturalized in dry coastal and inland tropical areas worldwide. The spiral seed pods are characteristic — opening to reveal black seeds embedded in a thin layer of sweet, white to pink, edible aril that tastes mildly of coconut and cream. The aril is eaten fresh by children and used in beverages, ice cream, and sweets throughout Mexico, Central America, and Southeast Asia where it has naturalized. The tree is exceptionally drought tolerant, thriving on shallow, rocky, alkaline soils where few other fruit trees survive. In zone 9a it may take frost damage but regrows vigorously. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen and is used as a windbreak, shade tree, and firewood tree in semi-arid tropical regions.
